claims.claim_text: An unattributed post-incident review found that ServiceAccount tokens and DNS-name certificates did not prove workload intent.
claims.span_hint: line 32 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: The post-incident review exposed a subtle but common platform failure. Kubernetes ServiceAccount tokens identified a Kubernetes subject, but they did not prove that a specific workload process was the intended caller of a specific service. Certificates existed in the environment too, but they were issued manually, rotated inconsistently, and tied to DNS names instead of workload intent. The team could encrypt traffic, but it could not confidently answer the more important question: "Which workload is this, and should this workload be allowed to make this request?"
claims.suggested_rewrite: The failure pattern is subtle: Kubernetes ServiceAccount tokens identify a Kubernetes subject, but they do not prove that a specific workload process is the intended caller of a specific service. Certificates may exist in the environment too, but when they are issued manually, rotated inconsistently, and tied to DNS names instead of workload intent, the team can encrypt traffic without confidently answering the more important question: "Which workload is this, and should this workload be allowed to make this request?"
claims.rationale: The sentence is framed as a real incident review but provides no source or attributable event, so the rewrite removes the incident framing while preserving the operational lesson.

claims.claim_text: SPIFFE defines SPIFFE IDs and SVIDs, while SPIRE implements attestation, issuance, rotation, and the Workload API runtime.
claims.span_hint: line 34 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: The SPIFFE README describes the standard concepts and identifies SPIRE as the runtime implementation for workload identity.

claims.claim_text: A SPIFFE ID is a URI containing a trust domain and path.
claims.span_hint: line 52 | section: 1. The Identity Problem SPIFFE Solves
claims.rationale: The SPIFFE ID standard defines the URI structure, trust domain, and path components.

claims.claim_text: X.509-SVIDs encode the SPIFFE ID as a URI Subject Alternative Name.
claims.span_hint: line 76 | section: 1. The Identity Problem SPIFFE Solves
claims.rationale: The X.509-SVID specification defines URI SAN encoding for SPIFFE IDs.

claims.claim_text: JWT-SVIDs are signed JWTs where the subject claim is the SPIFFE ID.
claims.span_hint: line 76 | section: 1. The Identity Problem SPIFFE Solves
claims.rationale: The JWT-SVID standard directly defines the subject claim as the SPIFFE ID.

claims.claim_text: Trust domains define signing authority and bundles, and federation makes trust between domains explicit.
claims.span_hint: line 84 | section: 1. The Identity Problem SPIFFE Solves
claims.rationale: The SPIFFE trust-domain and bundle standard defines trust domains, bundles, and federated bundles.

claims.claim_text: The SPIFFE Workload API is exposed over a local Unix domain socket and lets a workload obtain SVIDs and trust bundles without directly authenticating with a password.
claims.span_hint: line 86 | section: 1. The Identity Problem SPIFFE Solves
claims.rationale: The Workload API specification describes the local socket API, caller identification model, and SVID/bundle responses.

claims.claim_text: The SPIRE Server stores registration entries, signs SVIDs, manages bundles, and authenticates agents.
claims.span_hint: line 135 | section: 2. SPIRE Architecture from First Request to Issued Identity
claims.rationale: The SPIRE Server command and architecture documentation covers server responsibilities around entries, bundles, agents, and identity issuance.

claims.claim_text: SPIRE Kubernetes node attestation commonly uses projected ServiceAccount tokens and TokenReview validation.
claims.span_hint: line 135 | section: 2. SPIRE Architecture from First Request to Issued Identity
claims.rationale: The k8s_psat node attestor documentation describes projected ServiceAccount token validation through Kubernetes TokenReview.

claims.claim_text: The SPIRE Kubernetes workload attestor derives selectors from pod, namespace, ServiceAccount, label, and container metadata.
claims.span_hint: line 137 and lines 169-174 | section: 2. SPIRE Architecture from First Request to Issued Identity
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes workload attestor documentation enumerates the selector types and explains how SPIRE derives them.

claims.claim_text: SPIRE registration entries bind selectors to a SPIFFE ID under a parent identity.
claims.span_hint: line 167 | section: 2. SPIRE Architecture from First Request to Issued Identity
claims.rationale: The SPIRE Server entry documentation describes registration entries, selectors, SPIFFE IDs, and parent IDs.

claims.claim_text: The SPIFFE CSI driver exposes the SPIRE Agent Workload API socket to selected Kubernetes pods.
claims.span_hint: line 188 and line 284 | section: 3. Deploying SPIRE on Kubernetes
claims.rationale: The SPIFFE CSI README explains Workload API socket injection into pods and why it avoids direct hostPath mounting.

claims.claim_text: The `spire-agent api fetch x509` command fetches an X.509-SVID through the Workload API and can write certificate material with `-write`.
claims.span_hint: line 333 | section: 4. Registering Workloads and Inspecting X.509-SVIDs
claims.rationale: The SPIRE Agent command reference documents `api fetch x509`, `-socketPath`, and `-write` behavior.

claims.claim_text: An issued X.509-SVID certificate should contain the SPIFFE ID in the Subject Alternative Name URI field.
claims.span_hint: line 345 | section: 4. Registering Workloads and Inspecting X.509-SVIDs
claims.rationale: The X.509-SVID specification defines the URI SAN as the place where the SPIFFE ID is encoded.

claims.claim_text: The most common lab failure is a selector mismatch.
claims.span_hint: line 384 | section: 4. Registering Workloads and Inspecting X.509-SVIDs
claims.anchor_text: The most common lab failure is a selector mismatch.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A selector mismatch is a useful lab failure to recognize.
claims.rationale: The frequency claim is not sourced and is not necessary to teach the debugging pattern.

claims.claim_text: mTLS authentication alone is not authorization; services should validate the peer certificate and authorize the expected SPIFFE identity.
claims.span_hint: line 421 | section: 5. Using SPIFFE Identities for mTLS and Authorization
claims.rationale: Istio's security best practices distinguish authentication from authorization and recommend explicit authorization policy.

claims.claim_text: go-spiffe can stream X.509-SVIDs and bundles from the Workload API and use them in mTLS configuration with SPIFFE ID authorization.
claims.span_hint: line 477 | section: 5. Using SPIFFE Identities for mTLS and Authorization
claims.rationale: The go-spiffe README documents Workload API X.509 sources, mTLS helpers, authorization by SPIFFE ID, and streamed credential updates.

claims.claim_text: Istio can provide secure service-to-service communication, authorization, observability, and traffic management without application code changes.
claims.span_hint: line 479 and line 500 | sections: 5 and 6
claims.rationale: Istio's overview describes service-to-service security, policy, telemetry, and traffic-management capabilities without requiring application rewrites.

claims.claim_text: cert-manager automates certificate issuance, renewal, and use in Kubernetes.
claims.span_hint: line 500 | section: 6. Choosing SPIFFE/SPIRE Among Other Identity Tools
claims.rationale: The cert-manager project README states that it automates obtaining, renewing, and using certificates in Kubernetes.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes ServiceAccounts provide identities for processes running in pods and ServiceAccount tokens are used for Kubernetes API authentication.
claims.span_hint: line 46 and line 512 | sections: 1 and 6
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes ServiceAccount documentation explains ServiceAccounts for pod processes and token-based authentication to the API server.

claims.claim_text: SPIRE is designed to establish trust across heterogeneous platforms including Kubernetes, VMs, and other runtimes.
claims.span_hint: line 508 and line 592 | section: 6 and Did You Know
claims.rationale: The SPIRE repository describes SPIRE as supporting trust establishment across a wide variety of hosting platforms.

claims.claim_text: Agents may cache SVIDs and bundles, but new issuance, rotation, and registration changes depend on SPIRE Server availability and state.
claims.span_hint: line 607 and lines 659-664 | Common Mistakes and Quiz Question 5 answer
claims.rationale: The SPIRE Agent documentation covers local SVID and bundle handling, while server-backed issuance and entry state remain control-plane dependencies.

further_reading.title: SPIFFE Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary overview of SPIFFE workload identity concepts and the relationship between SPIFFE and SPIRE.

further_reading.title: SPIFFE ID Standard
further_reading.why_relevant: Defines the URI identity format, trust domain, and path conventions used throughout the module.

further_reading.title: SPIFFE Workload API
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative reference for local workload identity delivery through the Workload API.

further_reading.title: SPIRE Kubernetes Workload Attestor
further_reading.why_relevant: Documents the Kubernetes selectors that registration entries use to bind pod metadata to identities.

further_reading.title: go-spiffe
further_reading.why_relevant: Shows how applications consume SVIDs and bundles for SPIFFE-aware mTLS and identity authorization.

notes: The shared section pool currently has no SPIFFE/SPIRE source entries, so supported claims use proposed URLs. Code blocks, lab commands, diagrams, quiz prompts, and repeated Common Mistakes statements were not emitted separately unless they carried an unsupported factual claim or introduced a distinct source-backed behavior.
